title: New seminar series to explore interdisciplinary approaches in conservation research
content:
Last Tuesday 12th of May, we launched a new seminar series organized by the Global Change and Conservation lab. The series aims at showcasing cutting‑edge research on biodiversity conservation and human–nature relations, bringing together researchers from natural sciences, social sciences, humanities, and beyond.

Using a hybrid format and a rotating venue between three campuses (Viikki, Kumpula and City Center), we want to create a platform that fosters dialogue between natural sciences, social sciences and beyond, emphasizing the growing importance of interdisciplinary approaches in conservation research.

The first session was a success, with more than 50 attendees in person and 30 online. Aina Brias‑Guinart opened the session with insights into how recreation cultures evolve in protected areas, while Miquel Torrents‑Ticó shared his work on human–carnivore coexistence in Kenyan pastoral landscapes.

Meri Mononen‑Matias, Doctoral Researcher at the Faculty of Educational Sciences, shared her appreciation:

“Thank you for today's seminar — the presentations […] sparked exactly the kind of interdisciplinary thinking your series is aiming for.”

creator: lucasb@helsinki.fi

title: Hyvin pidetty -näyttely kertoo rakkaudesta elävään kaupunkiin
content:
Helsinki born and based printmaker Aino Sinda’s exhibition Hyvin pidetty (Well Kept) tells a story of the surrounding city the artist loves so much. The woodcut and intaglio prints on display are a story of daytime walks of a fulfilled, happy woman. When wandering around aimlessly the city is dense, everything wonderful is close by and the Kaisaniemi botanic garden is one of many places to calm down, get excited and live. Name of the exhibition refers to the need for care shared by gardens, cities and people alike.

Well Kept is on view at the Gallery Corridor of the Kaisaniemi Greenhouses from 16 June to 23 August 2026.

Research groups, artists, photographers, authors, nature hobbyists and all other nature enthusiasts are invited to propose an exhibition for the exhibition spaces of the Natural History Museum and the Kaisaniemi and Kumpula Botanic Gardens.

Kaisaniemenranta 2, Helsinki

title: Yhteiskunnallisen turvallisuuden tutkimuksen päivässä korostettiin poikkitieteellisyyttä
content:
Helsingin yliopistossa järjestettiin 20.5. ensimmäinen yhteiskunnallisen turvallisuuden tutkimuksen päivä, joka kokosi yhteen yliopiston tutkijoita yli kampusrajojen keskustelemaan ajankohtaisesta yhteiskunnallisen turvallisuuden tutkimuksesta. Tilaisuudessa kuultiin puheita Helsingin yliopiston ansioituneilta tutkijoilta ja sekä esillä oli laaja kattaus postereita. 

Sessioiden sarjan aloitti professori Johanna Lindahl lääketieteen ja eläinlääketieteen tiedekunnista sekä professori Marko Virta maatalous-metsätieteellisestä tiedekunnasta puhumalla lajienvälisistä kohtaamisista. Keskusteluissa zoonoosien ja antibioottiresistenssin kaltaisten laaja-alaisten ongelmien ratkaisemiseksi painotettiin tarvetta purkaa vakiintuneita käytänteitä ja raivata tietä monitieteiselle yhteistyölle. Lisäksi pohdittiin kuinka oikea tieto tavoittaa yleisön disinformaation keskellä.

creator: varjoviv@helsinki.fi

title: What's happening at ENS?
content:
Centre for European Studies staff members Hanna, Johanna, and Kaius participated in the Faculty’s conferment ceremony last week, marking an important academic tradition. This prestigious event, which is held only once every five years, celebrates scholarly achievement and the academic community, and it will next be organized in May 2031.

Looking ahead, there are also significant developments within the Centre. Kaius Tuori has been appointed as the new Director of the Finnish Institute in Rome, located at Villa Lante, for a four-year term beginning in autumn 2026. To replace Kaius' position, a new university lecturer will be hired to take on teaching duties in the European history and politics module of ENS.

title: Four years of EU-funded research: what it takes to make agricultural trade more sustainable in social, economic and environmental
content:
The special issue brings together findings from MATS (Making Agricultural Trade Sustainable) and a second thematically related Horizon 2020 project, TRADE4SD. Together, the two consortia produced eight articles covering trade governance, EU policy, African value chains, and the role of economic modelling in sustainability assessments.

Professor Bodo Steiner of the Department of Economics and Management served as coordinator of the MATS project.

A central message running through the special issue is that trade liberalisation alone is not enough to advance the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Effective governance mechanisms, including functioning dispute settlement systems, sustainability impact assessments prior to signing trade agreements, and enforceable sustainability standards where relevant stakeholders are consulted, are needed to translate trade into positive outcomes for people and the environment.

title: Kuvitus jakaa tietoa -näyttely esittelee tieteellisen kuvittamisen tekniikoita
content:
Maria Leskinen’s illustrations will be showcased at the Natural History Museum café from June 15 to August 16, 2026, in the exhibition Illustration Shares Knowledge. The exhibition explores the captivating world of scientific illustration, merging artistry with research. Featured species include familiar ones as well as a charming capybara from Brazil, an urban burrowing owl, and the newly discovered Kamping Poi bent-toed gecko from Cambodia. The display includes detailed illustrations and infographics, some created with the help of a microscope, showcasing a range of techniques from traditional drawing to digital art.

Illustration Shares Knowledge will be on display at the Natural History Museum café from June 15 to August 16, 2026.

The café follows the museum's opening hours and opens half an hour after the museum itself. Admission to the café is free.

Research groups, artists, photographers, writers, nature enthusiasts, and others interested in nature are welcome to propose exhibitions for the Natural History Museum and the botanical gardens in Kaisaniemi and Kumpula.

Pohjoinen Rautatiekatu 13, Helsinki

title: Art & Science Award for Office for Tree Migration
content:
The award recognizes pioneering collaborations at the intersection of technology, industry, art, and the creative sectors.

Office for Tree Migration (OTM), founded by Meyer-Brandis, was established at the Hyytiälä Forest Station in 2021. The OTM is a long-term project observing tree migration: a global phenomenon which has been studied as part of climate change by natural scientists in Finland and elsewhere. The filming of wandering Scots Pine has been going on non-stop in Siikaneva peatland in Ruovesi since February 2022.

This long-term project has been developed in close collaboration with the  at  the  at the University of Helsinki, the , and many other scientific institutions around the world. Agnes was the first artist-in-residency at Hyytiälä Forest Station in 2013 and have continued her dialogue with aerosol, forest, peatland and tree scientists since then.
snippet: The award recognizes pioneering collaborations at the intersection of technology, industry, art, and the creative sectors.
creator: mkulmala@helsinki.fi

title: Aleksanteri Conference 2026: registration now open
content:
The registration for presenting and non-presenting participants is now open for the 25th Annual Aleksanteri Conference "Mapping Security in Central and Eastern Europe and Eurasia: Fault Lines and Frontlines"! Please find the instructions on the conference website below.

In the 21st century, we live in a world that is increasingly insecure and wrought with wars and conflicts. Many global challenges—ranging from rising inequality to climate change—contribute to the proliferation of conflicts and pose major risks to states and societies, which threatens European and global security. Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine and the further rise of global turmoil challenges fundamental assumptions about international order, alliance politics, and regional dynamics and has led to a remarkable transformation of European defence. The new era of insecurity aggravates potential devastating consequences for societies, individuals and regions.

creator: turmaija@helsinki.fi

title: Puutarhan parhaat palat – Kesän maksuttomat opastukset Kaisaniemessä
content:
On a guided tour, you’ll journey through millions of years of evolution along the paths of the Evolution Garden, and immerse yourself in a world of scents and colours in the Garden of the senses. You’ll also admire unique lichen and moss gardens, while hearing stories about the garden’s past and present, and the buildings found in the area.

Tours will take place from June 23 to August 22, 2026. Free entrance, no prior registration required.

  • Tuesdays, Fridays and Saturdays at 15 (3 pm)

    • The tour starts at the greenhouse entrance and lasts approximately 40 minutes.

    Kaisaniemenranta 2, Helsinki

title: Arkadiankadun siltaremontti aiheuttaa muutoksia liikennejärjestelyihin Luonnontieteellisen museon lähistöllä
content:
The renovation will start with the construction of temporary traffic arrangements from Wednesday 7 January onwards. The renovation is expected to be completed in late 2026.

A temporary bridge for pedestrians will be built on the east side of Arkadiankatu over the Baana. The aim is to maintain the pedestrian route on the east side of the bridge until the temporary bridge is operational. Cyclists will also be able to use the bridge.

The Baana route underneath the bridge is expected to remain open for most of the renovation's duration, with some minor exceptions.

There will be changes to the motor vehicle routes by the bridge and around it in January. If possible, should be favoured during the bridge renovation.

Connections to the bus terminals in Kamppi will remain available, but there will be a slight diversion of routes.

Trams 1 and 1T will run via Simonkatu and Kamppi. Lasipalatsi and the Natural History Museum will not be served.

The diversion is due to the renovation of the Arkadiankatu bridge and will remain in place until late 2026. The renovation will not affect HSL's bus services. 

Pohjoinen Rautatiekatu 13

title: Ukrainan sota - yliopiston suositukset
content:
The University of Helsinki has condemned the military action by Russia and expressed its support for Ukraine and its university community. We have also expressed our support for Ukraine, together with other Finnish universities, through statements by and the . As a demonstration of support, we have illuminated buildings in the colours of the Ukrainian flag and raised the flag on our premises.

One of the principles of the University of Helsinki is to promote international collaboration in research and higher education, and to support these efforts even in difficult circumstances. Russia’s indefensible military aggression in Ukraine has required exceptional measures, which include those taken in the collaboration between universities.

The University of Helsinki is monitoring both national and EU-level sanctions, adapting our policies and processes accordingly. Together with other Finnish and international universities, the University of Helsinki strives to promote the end of military action in Ukraine, while supporting Ukrainian institutions and people.

title: City of Helsinki Scientific Award awarded to Academy Professor Minna Palmroth
content:
, the recipient of the 2026 City of Helsinki Scientific Award, is an Academy Professor at the University of Helsinki Faculty of Science. She is a space physics researcher of exceptional international stature, whose work has contributed significantly to the understanding of near-Earth space, space weather and issues related to the sustainable use of space.

Palmroth's most significant breakthroughs include the Vlasiator simulation, the world's first and most accurate six-dimensional hybrid Vlasov model of the Earth's magnetic environment, which is widely used in international space research.

From 2018 to 2025, Palmroth headed the Research Council of Finland's Centre of Excellence in Research of Sustainable Space, the research of which combined scientific excellence with societal impact. Palmroth is currently heading the Centre of Excellence in Space Resilience (2026–2033), which is conducting research to improve society's resilience in the constantly changing circumstance regarding the utilisation of near-Earth space.

title: Kaupunkiympäristö muovaa bakteeristoa, ja sillä voi olla vaikutusta terveyteemme
content:
Urbanisation shapes bacterial communities in ways that can be reflected in human health and the immune system. Juulia Manninen’s doctoral thesis explores this theme, specifically the effects of urban features on both the environment and human microbiota.

The study found that rubber mats commonly used in playgrounds and daycare centres hosted considerably less diverse and stable bacterial communities than equally bare and nutrient-poor natural rock surfaces. The finding suggests that the use of artificial materials may have a greater impact on children’s microbial exposure than previously thought. In children, early microbial exposure is particularly important for the development of the immune system.

The practices for maintaining urban green spaces also seem to make a difference. The less intensively managed parks studied, where grass is allowed to grow longer, harboured more diverse microbiota starting from the park entrances. Most park visitors pass through these sites, potentially exposing themselves to beneficial microbes.
